title: Chapter 26: The Armory
content: The armory was a gamer's fever dream.

I'd expected guns—maybe a few pistols, some ammunition, the kind of ordnance that a prepper would consider adequate. What I found was a wall-to-wall arsenal that would have made a militia weep with envy.

Rifles first: two AR-15 platforms with red-dot sights and angled foregrips, a bolt-action Remington 700 with a scope that could read license plates at 800 meters, and a short-barreled shotgun with a pistol grip that looked like it had been designed by someone who played too many video games and loved every minute of it.
Handguns: three 9mm Glocks with extended magazines, a .45 caliber 1911 with walnut grips, and a compact .380 that was small enough to fit in an ankle holster.
Melee: two tactical tomahawks, a kukri machete, and ariot baton that extended from six inches to two feet with a flick of the wrist.
And ammunition. *Boxes* of it. Enough to sustain a small war.
Mia stood in the middle of the armory with an expression I'd never seen on her face before—awe, pure and simple, the face of someone who'd found the Holy Grail and it was made of brass and gunpowder.

"The ARs are mil-spec," she said, running her fingers along the receiver with the tenderness of a violinist with a Stradivarius. "The Remington is a custom job—bedded action, match-grade barrel. And this..." She picked up the .45 1911 and worked the slide. "This is a Wilson Combat. Twelve hundred dollars, pre-apocalypse. Someone in the system has *taste*."
"Can you use all of it?"
"I can use most of it." She set down the 1911 and turned to face me. "But I can't use it alone. You need to learn."
"I've never fired a gun in my life."
"Then it's time to start."
The safe house had a training room.

I didn't know this—another feature that had unlocked with the armory, a space behind the medical bay that I'd assumed was storage. Instead, it was a thirty-foot shooting gallery with reactive targets, a rubber floor, and a ventilation system that whisked away gun smoke like it was never there.

Mia started me on the compact .380—"Your wrists aren't strong enough for anything bigger yet"—and the first two hours were the most frustrating, humiliating, and ultimately educational hours of my life.
"Grip. Stance. Breathe. Squeeze, don't pull. Front sight, target, front sight. Again."
I missed the target entirely on my first shot. The bullet went into the backstop with a pathetic *thwack* that seemed to echo with judgment.
"Again."
Second shot: the edge of the target. Barely a hit.
"Again."
Third shot: center mass.
"There," Mia said, and I heard something in her voice that might have been approval. "You're not hopeless. Just untrained. Untrained can be fixed."

She worked me through the fundamentals for three hours, until my shoulders ached and my ears rang despite the protective gear she'd found in the armory supply cabinet. By the end of it, I could hit a man-sized target at fifteen feet with reasonable consistency—not expert, not even competent, but functional. Good enough to not shoot myself or my allies.

"You'll practice every day," Mia said. "Thirty minutes, minimum. The muscle memory needs to be automatic—when the adrenaline hits, your fine motor control goes to hell, and all you have left is repetition."
"How long until I'm... good?"

"A year, maybe. Two until you're *actually* good. But you don't need to be good—you need to be not-dead, and for that, consistent hits at close range will do." She unloaded the .380 and handed it to me grip-first. "This is yours now. Keep it on you at all times."

I took the gun. It was lighter than I expected, and heavier than I wanted—both physically and metaphorically. I'd never owned a weapon before. Never wanted to. The gamer in me had always preferred swords and magic, the clean abstraction of fantasy violence.
But this was reality, and in reality, the dead didn't respawn, and the gun in my hand was the difference between protecting my people and failing them.
"Thank you," I said.
"Thank me by not shooting yourself in the foot." She turned to leave, then paused. "Alex. You asked me why I came down from the roof. You want to know the real reason?"
"Tell me."

"Because you were the first person I saw who was trying to build something instead of just surviving. The system gives you food and weapons and walls, but you're using them to create a *home*. That's different. That's worth protecting." She touched the Remington on the wall. "I'll protect it with you. For as long as it matters."
She left. I stood alone in the shooting gallery, a gun in my hand and a promise in my heart.
The system was giving me tools. It was up to me to use them.
For the first time since the outbreak, I felt ready.

**SYSTEM LEVEL UPDATE**
**LEVEL 2 — CHARMER**
**NEW ABILITY: Party Coordination — System can track and optimize group dynamics in real-time**
**NEXT LEVEL: 3 completed romances (Current: 0/3)**
Level 2. New abilities. Progress.

The numbers were climbing—romance percentages, system levels, body counts, survival days. All the metrics of a life that had started with a gamer sitting alone in his apartment and somehow turned into something that mattered.
I holstered the .380 and went to find my people.
They were worth fighting for.
I was going to make sure they knew it.
